subject: Physics
chapter: Chapter: Keeping Time with the Skies

key_points: Time measurement in early civilizations was based on observations of the Sun, Moon, and stars
The rotation of the Earth causes day and night and forms the basis of a 24-hour day
The revolution of the Earth around the Sun determines the length of a year
The phases of the Moon repeat in a regular cycle of about 29.5 days, forming the basis of lunar months
Celestial observations helped ancient people develop calendars and track seasons
examples: Using the position of the Sun to estimate the time of day
A sundial using the shadow of a stick to measure time
Tracking the phases of the Moon to determine months
Using constellations to identify seasons and directions
formulas: 1 day ≈ Time taken by Earth to complete one rotation (24 hours)
1 year ≈ Time taken by Earth to complete one revolution around the Sun (365 days)
Lunar month ≈ Time between two full moons (about 29.5 days)
exam_tips: Remember the difference between Earth’s rotation and revolution
Understand how the Sun, Moon, and stars helped measure time
Use examples like sundials and lunar calendars in explanations
Relate astronomical movements to days, months, and years
